lecture_context.lecture_title: Stanford CS231N Lecture 2: Image Classification with Linear Classifiers

unit.name: Linear classifier limitations, loss functions, and softmax
unit.description: Shows where linear separability fails, motivates a loss objective for learning good weights, and derives the softmax probability model and its negative log-likelihood loss.
unit.summary: The segment first shows failure cases for linear classifiers: XOR-like quadrant patterns, ring-shaped class regions, and multi-modal classes cannot be separated by one linear decision boundary [ts=3248s][ts=3258s][ts=3268s][ts=3284s]. It then states the learning problem: choose W by defining a loss or objective that measures unhappiness on training scores and then optimize W to minimize it [ts=3314s][ts=3327s][ts=3349s][ts=3361s]. The lecture instantiates this with the softmax classifier: raw scores are exponentiated to make them positive and then normalized to sum to one, yielding class probabilities [ts=3498s][ts=3533s][ts=3550s][ts=3559s][ts=3572s]. For a single example, the loss is the negative log of the correct-class probability [ts=3661s][ts=3675s][ts=3720s][ts=3730s]. The instructor also connects this same objective to maximum likelihood estimation, KL-divergence matching, and cross-entropy under one-hot labels [ts=3748s][ts=3803s][ts=3834s][ts=3862s]. Finally, the warm-start diagnostic is derived: if scores are initially nearly equal across C classes, the softmax loss is log C, which is about 2.3 for C=10 [ts=3978s][ts=4000s][ts=4009s].

key_points.text: Some class layouts are not linearly separable, including XOR-like quadrants, rings, and multi-modal regions.
Learning W requires a loss function that quantifies how bad the current scores are on training data.
Softmax converts raw scores into probabilities by exponentiating and normalizing.
For one example, softmax loss is the negative log of the correct-class probability.
The same objective can be viewed as maximum likelihood, KL-divergence minimization, or cross-entropy with one-hot labels.
If all C class probabilities are initially equal, the softmax loss is log(C), about 2.3 when C=10.

kp_catalog.name: Linear classifier as a parametric score function
kp_catalog.description: A learned affine map f(x,W)=Wx+b that converts a flattened image into class scores.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because linear classifier as a parametric score function formalizes how a learned affine map f(x,W)=Wx+b that converts a flattened image into class scores, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Limits of linear separability and the need for an objective
kp_catalog.description: Some label layouts cannot be separated by a single hyperplane, so learning requires a loss function that scores how well the current parameters behave on training data.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because limits of linear separability and the need for an objective clarifies some label layouts cannot be separated by a single hyperplane, so learning requires a loss function that scores how well the current parameters behave on training data, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Cross-entropy loss and negative log likelihood
kp_catalog.description: Transform raw class scores into probabilities with softmax and learn by minimizing the negative log probability of the correct class, equivalent here to maximum likelihood and cross-entropy under one-hot labels. For one-hot targets, cross entropy reduces to the model's log probability of the correct class and is optimized as negative log likelihood.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because cross-entropy loss and negative log likelihood formalizes how transform raw class scores into probabilities with softmax and learn by minimizing the negative log probability of the correct class, equivalent here to maximum likelihood and cross-entropy under one-hot labels, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context though importance labels split between critical and high.
